The Cornell Box

Part five: the picture above is being computed by gambling. Every pixel fires random rays into the room and averages what they find; the static you see at first is the honest uncertainty of an estimate mid-converge. Wait, and watch noise become light.

Scroll down to read ↓

Part four ended in debt. Soft shadows from near misses, skylight from a dot product, darkness from five probes — fast lies standing in for a truth nobody had stated yet. Here is the truth, from James Kajiya in 1986, and it fits in a line: 

L(x, ω) = Le(x, ω) + ∫ f·L(x', ω')·cosθ dω'   // leaving = emitted + arriving, over all directions

The light leaving any point is what it emits plus the integral, over every incoming direction, of the light arriving — which is itself light leaving somewhere else. The equation contains itself. Unrolled, it is an integral over every path light could possibly take through the scene: every bounce count, every route, every detour off the red wall. Two parts of this series solved it in 2D by hierarchy. In 3D, the integral's dimensionality explodes — and the tool that survives the explosion is the strangest one in numerical analysis.

Step 1 · Monte CarloIntegration by gambling

To integrate a function you cannot afford to evaluate everywhere, evaluate it somewhere, at random — and weight the answer honestly. The average of many random evaluations converges to the true integral; that is the law of large numbers wearing a lab coat. For the rendering equation, "evaluate at random" means: follow one random path of light, all the way through its bounces, and report what it found.

The estimate's error has a famous, infuriating shape: variance falls as 1/N, so the noise falls as 1/√N. Four times the samples buys half the grain. You can watch that law in person:

Hit pause early, while the room is still a blizzard. That frozen frame is what a handful of dice rolls knows about the scene — some pixels got lucky paths, most didn't. Resume and the law grinds forward: smooth at 64 samples, refined at 256, and the last subtle gradients still creeping in past 4,000. Nothing about the algorithm changes from first sample to last. It is the same gamble, repeated, averaged.

Aside · The box itself

The Cornell box is a real object — built at Cornell in 1984 to test whether rendered images could match a photograph of the same scene, radiometer readings and all. Red left wall, green right wall, white everything else: chosen so that bounced color would be unmistakable. It worked, and the geometry became the test pattern of global illumination. The aside in part two about radiosity? Same lab, same box, same year.

Step 2 · Better diceNext-event estimation

Pure random bouncing has a weakness you can compute in your head: if the lamp covers a thousandth of the directions a bounce could take, only a thousandth of paths ever find it, and every other path returns zero. The fix is not more samples — it is better questions. At every diffuse bounce, additionally send one deliberate ray straight at the lamp and ask "are you visible from here?", weighting the answer by the lamp's solid angle. The random walk continues as before; it just stops being the only source of information. 

at every bounce:  radiance += throughput · Le · (visibility · geometry / pdf)
// one shadow ray per bounce — the same dice, loaded toward where the light is

The lamp here is deliberately small, and the toggle is brutal: with light sampling off, the room at one second is a cave of fireflies — bright speckles where a lucky path stumbled into the lamp. With it on, the same one second of work produces a smooth, plausible room. Same scene, same number of rays, same honest average — the variance went into a different variable. The general principle is called importance sampling, and the modern renderer is mostly a tower of it.

Step 3 · Paying the debtBounce by bounce

Part four faked three things: soft shadows, ambient light, and contact darkness. Watch all three stop being fake, one bounce at a time:

At one bounce the box is direct light only — hard geometry of lit and unlit, the ceiling black because no lamp shines up. Two bounces and the debt starts clearing: the red wall bleeds onto the white sphere, the ceiling glows with floor-light, shadows fill with ambience that comes from somewhere instead of a constant. By six, the corners hold that soft contact darkness AO could only impersonate — emerging here as a statistical fact about how little light survives into a crevice. Every phenomenon parts one through four built a separate mechanism for is present in this one loop, as a consequence.

Step 4 · AssemblyThe box as instrument

The full toy. Drag to carry the lamp — and notice the renderer's honesty: the moment anything moves, the accumulated past becomes a lie and the image restarts from static. Shrink the light to a point and shadows go knife-edged; stretch it wide and they melt, the soft shadow part four approximated with one clever multiply now arising from actual area, actually sampled. Set the sphere to mirror and check its reflection: the red wall, the lamp, the other sphere — a whole second rendering for free, because a mirror is just a bounce with no dice.

Glossy is the knob worth lingering on. At roughness zero it is the mirror; nudge it and the reflection blurs exactly the way the shadow blurred when the lamp grew — and for exactly the same reason. A rough surface is an area light's worth of mirror directions. By the end of this series every blur in an image has been the same object: an integral, seen from a different chair.

CodaWhat the dice still owe

This is the algorithm behind every film frame rendered this century — Kajiya's equation, Monte Carlo, and forty years of increasingly loaded dice. What the browser tab version lacks is mostly patience: production tracers denoise, reuse, and importance-sample their way to the same average faster, but the average they estimate is this one.

One absence is conspicuous. Everything in this box is opaque. No glass, no water, no lens — nothing that bends light rather than bouncing it. Refraction is where the path tracer starts producing the phenomena that made people fall in love with optics in the first place: caustics, focus, and the fact — concealed so far by every white-ish light in this series — that the bending depends on wavelength. The last part hands the renderer a prism, and color stops being three numbers.

Light in a Browser Tab, part five · part one: The Lava Lamp · part two: The Bonfire · part three: The Fog · part four: Leaving Flatland

Standing on the shoulders of: Kajiya, "The Rendering Equation" (1986) · Goral, Torrance, Greenberg & Battaile (1984) — the box · Cook, Porter & Carpenter, "Distributed Ray Tracing" (1984) · Veach, "Robust Monte Carlo Methods for Light Transport Simulation" (1997) · Pharr, Jakob & Humphreys, "Physically Based Rendering" (2004–)
